name: Ame-no-Koyane
shortDescription: Ame-no-Koyane is a Shinto deity associated with writing, literature, and divination. He played a crucial role in coaxing Amaterasu out of the Ama-no-Iwato cave.
description: Ame-no-Koyane is a prominent kami in Japanese mythology, revered as a deity of writing, literature, and divination. He is best known for his pivotal role in the myth of Ama-no-Iwato (the Rock Cave of Heaven). When the sun goddess Amaterasu retreated into the cave, plunging the world into darkness, Ame-no-Koyane, along with other deities, devised a plan to lure her out. He chanted sacred prayers and recited divine words (norito) before the cave entrance, creating a sacred space that would entice Amaterasu to emerge. His powerful words, combined with the boisterous dance of Ame-no-Uzume, the crowing of the eternal rooster Tokoyo-no-Naganakidori, and the other deities' actions, ultimately succeeded in drawing Amaterasu out of the cave, restoring light to the world. Ame-no-Koyane is considered the ancestor of the Nakatomi clan, who were historically responsible for performing important Shinto rituals at the Imperial court. His association with writing and sacred pronouncements underscores his importance in the transmission and preservation of religious knowledge. He is often depicted holding a writing brush or scroll, symbolizing his role as a scribe and communicator between the divine and mortal realms. His presence is invoked during important ceremonies and festivals, particularly those related to purification (harae) and communication with the kami.
